title: AML Agents Briefing — Thursday, 04 June 2026
script:
ALEX: Welcome to AML Agents Briefing for Wednesday the fourth of June 2026. This is your audio overview of the most material AML and financial crime developments across Asia-Pacific today. Full detail, your obligation register, source links, and horizon scanning are all at amlagents dot streamlit dot app.

JORDAN: Big day, Alex. We've got the Reserve Bank of Australia's Payments System Board meeting outcomes, Google's new deepfake call detection feature and what it means for fraud teams, and a quick read on the Santander and G42 artificial intelligence deal. Let's get into it.

ALEX: Starting with Australia. The Reserve Bank of Australia's Payments System Board held its June 2026 meeting yesterday, and the outcomes carry real operational weight for compliance teams managing transaction monitoring and real-time payment risk controls. The context here matters: Australia's AML and CTF Act reforms under Tranche 2 are already reshaping obligations across the sector, and real-time payment volumes are climbing. Any Board-level signal about payment system oversight feeds directly into how AUSTRAC-supervised entities need to frame their monitoring frameworks.

JORDAN: So what's the practical read for listeners on this?

ALEX: The Board's focus on payment system stability and fraud mitigation aligns closely with AUSTRAC's own supervisory priorities around suspicious matter reporting timeliness. We've already seen a forty-seven million Australian dollar civil penalty against a major digital currency exchange for systemic failures in transaction monitoring and suspicious matter report timeliness. The Board meeting reinforces that regulators are looking end to end, from payment rails through to your AML controls.

JORDAN: And the action item here is pretty clear. If you're a compliance officer or MLRO at a payments institution or a digital currency exchange operating under AUSTRAC supervision, now is the moment to pressure-test your real-time payment monitoring rules. Check whether your transaction monitoring system flags velocity anomalies on inbound and outbound flows within the same settlement window. The DCE enforcement case is a direct template for what AUSTRAC examiners will look for on their next visit.

ALEX: Shifting focus now to something that will be on every fraud team's radar this week. Google has introduced an on-device feature for Android that detects AI-generated voice deepfakes during live telephone calls, flagging suspected impersonation in real time. For financial institutions, this matters because voice-based social engineering and authorised push payment fraud are two of the fastest-growing fraud vectors across the Asia-Pacific region.

JORDAN: That's a significant development. So how does this change the equation for financial crime teams?

ALEX: The technology itself is promising, but the compliance implication runs in both directions. On one hand, your customer-facing fraud prevention posture can start incorporating whether customers are using updated Android devices as a mitigating factor in your channel risk model. On the other hand, fraudsters will adapt. They'll move to channels or devices where detection doesn't exist.

JORDAN: Right, and this connects directly to the Bank Negara Malaysia typology bulletin from Q1 2026 on investment scams routed through digital banks. Mule accounts seeded via voice-based social engineering are a recurring theme in that bulletin. If you're heading fraud or financial crime at a digital bank or electronic money institution in Malaysia, Singapore, or Hong Kong, take this Android announcement as a prompt to review your voice channel controls and your customer authentication procedures for high-value outbound transfers. Document the review. It's exactly the kind of proactive step that satisfies HKMA guideline Section 11 expectations around self-assessment.

ALEX: Before we close, a word on Banco Santander's memorandum of understanding with Abu Dhabi-based technology group G42, announced yesterday. The deal signals Santander's intent to deploy large-scale AI infrastructure sourced from a Gulf state entity. For compliance leaders outside that deal, the relevance is around third-party and technology vendor risk.

JORDAN: Exactly. When a global bank sources AI infrastructure from a sovereign-linked technology group, the questions your senior leadership should be asking are about data residency, access controls, and whether your AI governance framework, which increasingly regulators in Hong Kong, Singapore, and Australia expect to see documented, covers third-party AI providers not just internal models. Raise this at your next risk committee. If your institution is in the middle of an AI deployment for transaction monitoring or customer risk scoring, confirm that your vendor due diligence covers the full ownership chain.

ALEX: What to watch in the coming week.

JORDAN: Three things. First, if you're an MLRO or AML compliance officer with operations in Indonesia, your annual independent review of your AML and CFT programme for the calendar year 2024 is due to your Risk Oversight Committee and Board of Commissioners by the thirtieth of June. That deadline is four weeks away. Confirm your internal audit team has signed off and that findings are documented in a form the Otoritas Jasa Keuangan expects under POJK 12 of 2017. Second, for AUSTRAC-supervised entities, AUSTRAC has now published detailed Tranche 2 guidance for legal practitioners, covering source of wealth diligence in conveyancing and suspicious matter report practicalities. Even if you're not a law firm, read it. It signals how AUSTRAC is thinking about designated service triggers across professional services sectors. Third, check your horizon for the Hong Kong Monetary Authority's annual self-assessment return under Section 11 of its AML and CFT Guideline. That return is due in September, and Internal Audit validation takes time to organise.

JORDAN: That's your briefing for the fourth of June. Everything we've covered today, including your full obligation register, source links, and horizon scan, is at amlagents dot streamlit dot app. Worth bookmarking if you haven't already.

ALEX: Thanks for listening. We'll be back tomorrow with the next update. Stay sharp.
